title: Working with the part that believes it caused the abuse
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part that carries the blame

You were small. Maybe very small. And something terrible happened to you. Something that should never have happened to any child. And now, decades later, there's a part of you that still believes you caused it. That you deserved it. That if you had been better, quieter, smarter, less needy, more perfect, it wouldn't have happened.

I know this part. I've sat with this part in my own body. It's not a bad part. It's not an enemy. It's a child who needed to make sense of an incomprehensible world. And the story it told itself was this: if I caused the abuse, then I have some control. If I caused it, then maybe I can stop it next time. If I caused it, then the world isn't random and terrifying. It's just that I'm bad. And if I can fix myself, I'll be safe.

Here's the thing. That logic kept you alive. It preserved your attachment to caregivers you depended on for survival. It gave you a story when there was no other story available. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how children will internalize blame rather than face the unbearable truth that their caregivers are unsafe. The alternative is annihilation. So the part takes the blame. It carries it. It protects you from something worse.

But that protection came at a cost. A brutal cost. That part now lives inside you, whispering that you're flawed. That you're toxic. That people will leave when they see the real you. That the abuse was your fault. And you've been trying to fix, fight, or banish this part for years. Maybe decades. And it's still there.

Because you can't banish a part that's trying to protect you. You can only learn to see it differently.

What the part actually believes

Let's get specific about what's happening inside. This part isn't stupid. It's not irrational. It's operating on a logic that made perfect sense in the context of your childhood. Think about it. If you were a child being hurt by an adult, you had two options. Option one: my parent is dangerous and I cannot trust them, which means I am completely alone and helpless in this world. Option two: I did something wrong, and if I fix it, I will be safe and loved.

I think about exile parts a lot. In IFS language, exiles are the young, wounded parts that carry the pain, the shame, the memories that the protector parts work so hard to keep locked away. They're the parts we're most afraid of. Because if we let them speak, if we let them be seen, we're afraid the pain will swallow us whole.

But here's what Richard Schwartz found in decades of clinical work. When exiles are finally witnessed - when someone (even your own Self) turns toward them with compassion instead of away from them with fear - they don't destroy you. They soften. They release the burdens they've been carrying. Sometimes for decades.

You don't have to do this alone. And you don't have to do it all at once. But I want you to know that those parts of you that feel too much, that carry too much, that seem too broken to touch - they're not too anything. They're just waiting. Waiting for someone to say "I see you. I'm not leaving. You don't have to carry this alone anymore." And sometimes, that someone can be you.

Which one could a child survive? Option two. Every time. The part chose option two because that was the only survivable choice. Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, calls this a "firefighter" part. It rushes in to put out the emotional fire with whatever story it can find. Even if that story burns you later.

So this part holds a belief that feels like absolute truth. It says: "I caused the abuse because I was too much. I was too sensitive. I asked for too much attention. I was bad. I was dirty. I was wrong." And that belief is locked in the body. It's not a thought you can reason away. It's a somatic experience. A felt sense of shame that lives in your chest, your throat, your gut.

Stay with me here. This is where most approaches fail. The wellness industry will tell you to "reframe" your thoughts. To write affirmations. To "choose" a different narrative. But that's like telling someone with a broken leg to just think their way into walking. The part doesn't need a new belief. It needs to be seen. It needs to be heard. It needs to be understood.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker gets this. He writes about the inner critic not as something to destroy, but as something to understand. A part that developed for a reason.

How to work with the part, not against it

So what do you actually do? You don't argue with this part. You don't tell it to shut up. You don't prove it wrong with evidence. Because arguing with a part that's been protecting you for thirty years is like yelling at a firefighter who's trying to save your house. They're not going to stop. They're going to work harder.

Instead, you slow down. Way down. You notice the part. You feel where it lives in your body. Maybe it's a tightness in your chest. Maybe a knot in your stomach. Maybe a voice that says "see, you're doing this wrong too." You don't fight it. You just notice. You say, "I see you there. I know you're trying to protect me. I'm not going to push you away."

This is not about bypassing. This is not toxic positivity. This is direct, honest engagement with the reality of your inner world. Janina Fisher, who writes extensively about trauma and the self, describes this as "minding the gap" between the part and the self. You create a tiny space of curiosity. You ask the part: what do you need me to know? What are you afraid would happen if you stopped believing this?

The answers will break your heart. The part is afraid that if it stops blaming you, the abuse will start again. It's afraid that if it releases the shame, there will be nothing left to hold you together. It's afraid that the alternative is facing how utterly helpless and betrayed you were. And that feels like dying.

So you don't force it. You don't demand the part change. You just sit with it. You let it know you're here now. You're an adult now. You have resources now that you didn't have then. You can keep it safe. For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link) by Jonice Webb. It speaks directly to the parts that learned to blame themselves for not getting their needs met.

The slow work of releasing shame

This is not quick. I want to be honest with you. The part that believes it caused the abuse is not going to disappear after one conversation. It's not going to vanish after a weekend workshop or a single therapy session. This is slow, layered work. You'll talk to the part. It will relax a little. Then something will trigger it, and it will come roaring back. That's not failure. That's how healing works.

Let me tell you about the protector parts. The ones that look like anger, or control, or perfectionism, or emotional walls. In IFS, we call them managers and firefighters. Managers try to prevent pain by controlling everything. Firefighters try to extinguish pain after it's already been triggered - through numbing, bingeing, dissociating, raging.

I used to hate my protector parts. The part that made me cold and distant. The part that made me work until I collapsed. The part that drank too much. I thought they were the problem. Turns out they were the solution - the best solution my system could find at the time. They kept me alive when nothing else could.

Notice if there's a part of you that's skeptical right now. A part that says "this is too simple" or "this won't work for me" or "I'm too damaged for this." That's a protector too. It's trying to save you from hope, because hope has been dangerous before. It's been followed by disappointment too many times. Can you thank that part? Can you say "I get it. You're trying to protect me from getting hurt again." That's not weakness. That's the beginning of internal trust.

Each time you approach the part with curiosity instead of judgment, you're rewiring a neural pathway. You're teaching your nervous system that this part can be seen without being believed. That the shame can be held without being acted on. That you can survive the discomfort of not knowing who to blame.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his research at the Trauma Center, showed that trauma lives in the body. It's not just a story in your head. So the release has to happen in the body too. That might mean shaking. Crying. Yawning. Feeling heat or cold in your hands. It might mean putting a hand on your chest and saying, "I'm here. I'm not leaving you."

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She was the first to name that the core of trauma is disconnection. Disconnection from yourself, from others, from meaning. The part that believes it caused the abuse is a symptom of that disconnection. And reconnection is the cure. Slow, patient, imperfect reconnection.

What happens when the part softens

I can't promise you that this part will ever fully go away. But I can tell you what happens when it softens. You start to notice a different voice. A quieter one. One that says, "Maybe it wasn't your fault. Maybe you were just a child. Maybe you deserved love and safety and never got it." That voice feels foreign at first. You might not trust it. You might think it's lying.

That's okay. You don't have to believe it yet. You just have to let it exist alongside the other part. Let them both be there. The part that says "I caused it" and the part that says "I was innocent." They can coexist. In fact, they have to. Because healing isn't about erasing one part and installing another. It's about creating enough internal space for all of you to exist.

Wild, right? You don't have to kill the part that blames you. You just have to stop feeding it with your attention and your fear. You have to turn toward it with compassion and say, "I see you. I know you're scared. But I'm in charge now. And I'm going to take care of us."

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you exercises to track these parts and build that internal relationship. It's not a replacement for therapy, but it's a companion for the journey.

You might notice something in your body right now. A shift. A relaxation. Or maybe a tightening. Both are okay. Both are information. The part is listening. It's watching to see if you're really going to show up differently this time. And you are. Not perfectly. Not all at once. But you're here. You're reading this. You're trying. And that's everything.

So keep going. Keep sitting with the part. Keep asking it what it needs. Keep telling it that you're here now, that you're not that child anymore, that you have resources and strength and the ability to protect it. And one day, maybe not today, but one day, that part will whisper back: "Maybe I can rest now. Maybe I don't have to carry this alone anymore."

And that's when the real healing begins.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Running on Empty (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
